---
title: "Latent Factor Models for Counterfactual Prediction"
author: "Codex"
date: "2026-03-28"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    page-layout: full
    code-fold: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Executive Summary

This note reviews latent-factor models for counterfactual prediction in panels
and panel-like matrices, and turns that review into a concrete problem
specification for `torchonometrics`.

The key point is that grouped fixed effects, interactive fixed effects, matrix
completion, and generalized low-rank GLMs are all variations on the same latent
index template:

$$
\eta_{it} = x_{it}'\beta + m_{it},
$$

where $m_{it}$ is a latent outcome surface.

Different methods correspond to different restrictions on $m_{it}$:

- additive FE: $m_{it} = \alpha_i + \lambda_t$;
- grouped FE: $m_{it} = a_{g(i),t}$;
- interactive FE: $m_{it} = a_i^\top b_t$;
- generalized matrix completion: $\mathbb E[Y_{it}\mid X] = g^{-1}(x_{it}'\beta + a_i^\top b_t)$.

Grouped FE is therefore a special case of low-rank modeling: it constrains the
row factors to be one-hot. Matrix completion is the continuous-factor analogue.

The implementation conclusion is straightforward:

> `torchonometrics` should eventually support a generic family of latent-factor
> GLM estimators of the form
> $g^{-1}(x_{it}'\beta + a_i^\top b_t)$,
> with grouped FE as an optional specialization.

PyTorch is a particularly good substrate for this because the practical solver
is almost always a factorized nonconvex program, and that is exactly the kind
of problem where GPU-backed autodiff and custom losses are useful.

## Motivation

Counterfactual prediction in panel data is fundamentally a missing-data problem.
We observe a subset of untreated outcomes and want to recover the latent
untreated surface on missing or treated cells.

Examples include:

- staggered adoption designs, where treated units are missing their untreated
  potential outcomes after treatment;
- synthetic-control and panel-counterfactual settings with partially observed
  untreated trajectories;
- panel recommendation or demand problems with many missing entries;
- structural models where the mean function is only partially observed under
  realized choices or states.

In all of these settings, the challenge is not only to estimate $\beta$, but to
reconstruct the latent heterogeneity surface in a way that generalizes
out-of-sample.

## Taxonomy of Models

### Additive Fixed Effects

The standard panel baseline is

$$
y_{it} = x_{it}'\beta + \alpha_i + \lambda_t + \varepsilon_{it}.
$$

This is robust and interpretable, but it assumes the latent surface is additive
in unit and time. That is often too rigid for counterfactual prediction.

### Grouped Fixed Effects

Bonhomme and Manresa (2015) propose

$$
y_{it} = x_{it}'\beta + a_{g(i),t} + \varepsilon_{it},
$$

where the group assignment $g(i)$ is latent and takes finitely many values.

Interpretation:

- units belong to a small number of latent archetypes;
- each archetype has its own time path;
- heterogeneity is discrete, not fully continuous.

This is attractive when:

- one believes in persistent types;
- stronger pooling is needed than unit FE provides;
- interpretability matters.

### Interactive Fixed Effects

Bai (2009) studies the continuous-factor analogue

$$
y_{it} = x_{it}'\beta + a_i^\top b_t + \varepsilon_{it},
$$

with rank $r$ small.

This allows continuous latent variation across units and richer time patterns
than additive FE.

### Generalized Matrix Completion / Latent-Factor GLMs

The most general version for this library is

$$
\mathbb E[Y_{it}\mid X_{it},A,B] = g^{-1}(x_{it}'\beta + a_i^\top b_t),
$$

or equivalently

$$
\min_{\beta,A,B}\sum_{(i,t)\in\Omega}
\ell(Y_{it}, x_{it}'\beta + a_i^\top b_t)
+
\mathcal R(A,B,\beta),
$$

where $\Omega$ is the observed set and $\ell$ is the appropriate GLM loss.

This nests:

- Gaussian interactive FE;
- Bernoulli-logit or probit factor models;
- Poisson factor models;
- matrix completion with observed covariates;
- grouped FE when $A$ is constrained to be one-hot.

## Grouped FE as a Constrained Low-Rank Model

Write

$$
M = AB^\top,
$$

where $M_{it}=m_{it}$.

Grouped FE is the special case where each row of $A$ is one of the basis
vectors:

$$
a_i \in \{e_1,\dots,e_G\}.
$$

Then $AB^\top$ has only $G$ distinct row-types. So grouped FE is best viewed as
a discrete low-rank model rather than something conceptually separate from
matrix completion.

This yields a clean preference rule:

- choose grouped FE when latent heterogeneity is genuinely type-like or when
  interpretability and strong pooling matter;
- choose continuous low rank when latent heterogeneity is smooth and prediction
  is the priority.

## Why These Models Matter for Counterfactual Prediction

Athey et al. (2021) explicitly connect matrix completion methods to causal panel
data models. Their perspective is useful because it makes the prediction target
transparent.

Let $\Omega_{\text{train}}$ be the observed untreated cells. Then the workflow
is:

1. estimate the latent untreated surface on $\Omega_{\text{train}}$;
2. validate on held-out untreated cells;
3. predict untreated potential outcomes on treated or missing cells;
4. form treatment effects by comparing observed treated outcomes to predicted
   untreated outcomes.

This is a prediction problem with causal interpretation layered on top. The
model does not identify treatment effects by itself; it imposes structure on
the untreated outcome surface.

## Bias-Variance Tradeoffs

### Grouped FE vs Unit FE

Suppose the truth is

$$
y_{it} = x_{it}'\beta + \alpha_{it} + \varepsilon_{it},
$$

and grouped FE approximates $\alpha_{it}$ by $a_{g(i),t}$. Define

$$
r_{it} = \alpha_{it} - a_{g(i),t}.
$$

Then the second-step regression sees

$$
y_{it} = x_{it}'\beta + a_{g(i),t} + r_{it} + \varepsilon_{it}.
$$

Residualizing by grouped FE gives the decomposition

$$
\hat\beta_{GFE} - \beta
=
(\tilde X'\tilde X)^{-1}\tilde X'r
+
(\tilde X'\tilde X)^{-1}\tilde X'\varepsilon.
$$

So:

- the first term is approximation bias;
- the second term is variance.

Grouped FE can beat unit FE in mean squared error when the reduction in
variance dominates the approximation bias. That is most plausible when latent
heterogeneity is truly low-dimensional or type-like.

### Grouped FE vs Continuous Low Rank

If the true latent surface is

$$
m_{it} = h_t(\theta_i)
$$

for a low-dimensional continuous type $\theta_i$, then:

- grouped FE approximates $h_t$ by a step function over type space;
- continuous low rank approximates it by a bilinear factorization.

That implies:

- grouped FE has stronger pooling and more interpretable structure;
- continuous low rank usually has lower approximation bias when heterogeneity is
  genuinely continuous;
- grouped FE may still win in finite samples if the true structure is close to
  clustered and the stronger shrinkage helps.

## Nonlinear Likelihoods: Where the Payoff Can Be Larger

The strongest motivation for grouped or low-rank latent heterogeneity is often
not linear panels but nonlinear likelihoods.

Examples:

- binary outcomes:
  $$
  \Pr(Y_{it}=1\mid X)=\Lambda(x_{it}'\beta + a_i^\top b_t)
  $$
- counts:
  $$
  \mathbb E[Y_{it}\mid X]=\exp(x_{it}'\beta + a_i^\top b_t)
  $$

With unrestricted unit FE:

- the nuisance dimension is large;
- finite-sample behavior can be unstable;
- incidental-parameter bias may contaminate common-parameter estimates when $T$
  is small or moderate.

Grouped or low-rank latent heterogeneity can help by shrinking the nuisance
space. Bonhomme, Lamadon, and Manresa (2022) emphasize exactly this broader
dimension-reduction role: discretization is a useful approximation device even
when heterogeneity is not literally discrete.

## Why PyTorch Is a Good Home for This

Yes: a PyTorch-based econometrics library is a natural place to implement these
solvers.

The practical optimization target is the factorized nonconvex problem

$$
\min_{\beta,A,B}
\sum_{(i,t)\in\Omega}
\ell(Y_{it}, x_{it}'\beta + a_i^\top b_t)
+
\lambda_A \|A\|_F^2
+
\lambda_B \|B\|_F^2,
$$

possibly with additional constraints or penalties.

PyTorch is well matched to this because:

- autodiff gives link-specific gradients essentially for free;
- GPU linear algebra matters once $N\times T$ gets large;
- sparse and masked losses are easy to express;
- first-order and quasi-Newton optimizers are already available;
- grouped, continuous, and hybrid constraints can share the same core code.

Convex nuclear-norm estimators remain important theoretically, but factorized
nonconvex solvers are usually the more practical first implementation in a
library like this.

## State of the Art: Broadly

There is no universal single best estimator. The landscape is segmented.

### Econometrics

- Bai (2009): interactive FE in linear panels.
- Chen, Fernández-Val, and Weidner (2021): nonlinear factor models for network
  and panel data.
- Bonhomme, Lamadon, and Manresa (2022): discrete approximations to unrestricted
  heterogeneity in nonlinear and structural settings.

This literature cares about:

- identification and normalization;
- incidental-parameter bias;
- large-$N$, large-$T$ asymptotics;
- interpretability of latent heterogeneity.

### Causal Panel Prediction

- Athey et al. (2021): matrix completion for causal panel counterfactuals.

This literature cares about:

- prediction error on held-out untreated cells;
- treatment-effect recovery;
- robustness to missingness patterns induced by policy timing.

### Statistics / Machine Learning

- Udell et al. (2016): generalized low-rank models.
- Fan, Gong, and Zhu (2019): generalized trace regression with nuclear-norm
  regularization.

This literature cares about:

- generalized losses beyond Gaussian settings;
- convex relaxations versus factorized nonconvex solvers;
- minimax rates and regularization theory.

The practical frontier is a hybrid:

- convex theory for guarantees and sometimes initialization;
- factorized nonconvex optimization for scalable deployment.

## Problem Specification for `torchonometrics`

The right build target is a generic latent-factor GLM module with grouped FE as
an optional constrained mode.

### Core Model

Support

$$
\mu_{it} = g^{-1}(x_{it}'\beta + a_i^\top b_t + o_{it}),
$$

where:

- $x_{it}$ are observed covariates;
- $o_{it}$ is an optional offset;
- $A\in\mathbb R^{N\times r}$ are unit factors;
- $B\in\mathbb R^{T\times r}$ are time factors.

### Families

At minimum:

- Gaussian
- Bernoulli-logit
- Poisson

### Observation Layout

Support both:

- dense panel tensors where appropriate;
- sparse observation lists `(unit_id, time_id, x_it, y_it)` with masks.

That is the right representation for counterfactual prediction problems.

### Estimator Modes

1. continuous low-rank factors
2. grouped FE with hard assignments
3. optional hybrid additive FE plus low rank

### Regularization

Start with:

- ridge penalties on $A$ and $B$;
- optional norm penalties on $\beta$;
- rank chosen by user, then later by validation.

### Solver Strategy

Use factorized nonconvex optimization as the default:

- Adam / AdamW for large problems;
- LBFGS for medium dense problems;
- optional alternating updates for grouped FE.

### Validation and Counterfactual Workflow

For causal use:

1. train only on untreated observed cells;
2. validate on held-out untreated cells;
3. choose rank or number of groups by predictive performance;
4. predict missing untreated outcomes;
5. aggregate treatment effects externally.

## Proposed API Sketch

```python
model = LatentFactorGLM(
    family="poisson",
    rank=4,
    penalty=1e-2,
    optimizer=torch.optim.AdamW,
    maxiter=2000,
    device="cuda",
)

model.fit(
    X=X,
    y=y,
    unit_ids=unit_ids,
    time_ids=time_ids,
    mask=train_mask,
    offset=offset,
)

mu_hat = model.predict_mean(X, unit_ids, time_ids)
eta_hat = model.predict_index(X, unit_ids, time_ids)
```

Grouped FE would be a constrained variant:

```python
model = GroupedFactorGLM(
    family="bernoulli",
    n_groups=8,
    classification="kmeans",
    maxiter=200,
)
```

## Milestones

### M1: Gaussian Latent-Factor Regression

Implement

$$
y_{it}=x_{it}'\beta + a_i^\top b_t + \varepsilon_{it}
$$

with masks, prediction on held-out cells, and ridge regularization.

### M2: Bernoulli and Poisson Latent-Factor GLMs

Extend the loss family while keeping the same factorized solver.

### M3: Grouped FE Mode

Add hard or soft assignment constraints so grouped FE becomes a specialization
of the generic latent-factor module.

### M4: Counterfactual Utilities

Add evaluation helpers specifically for train/validation/prediction splits on
untreated cells.

## Minimal Usage Sketch

```{python}
#| eval: false
import torch
from torchonometrics import LatentFactorGLM

model = LatentFactorGLM(
    family="bernoulli",
    rank=4,
    penalty=1e-2,
    optimizer=torch.optim.AdamW,
    device="cuda",
)

model.fit(
    X=X,
    y=y,
    unit_ids=unit_ids,
    time_ids=time_ids,
    mask=untreated_mask,
)

y0_hat = model.predict_mean(X, unit_ids, time_ids)
```

## References

- Bai, J. (2009). Panel Data Models with Interactive Fixed Effects. *Econometrica*, 77(4), 1229-1279.
- Bonhomme, S., and Manresa, E. (2015). [Grouped Patterns of Heterogeneity in Panel Data](https://doi.org/10.3982/ECTA11319). *Econometrica*, 83(3), 1147-1184.
- Bonhomme, S., Lamadon, T., and Manresa, E. (2022). [Discretizing Unobserved Heterogeneity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.02124). *Econometrica*, 90(2), 625-643.
- Athey, S., Bayati, M., Doudchenko, N., Imbens, G., and Khosravi, K. (2021). [Matrix Completion Methods for Causal Panel Data Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/1710.10251). *Journal of the American Statistical Association*, 116(536), 1716-1730.
- Chen, M., Fernández-Val, I., and Weidner, M. (2021). [Nonlinear Factor Models for Network and Panel Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/1412.5647). *Journal of Econometrics*, 220(2), 296-324.
- Udell, M., Horn, C., Zadeh, R., and Boyd, S. (2016). [Generalized Low Rank Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/1410.0342). *Foundations and Trends in Machine Learning*, 9(1), 1-118.
- Fan, J., Gong, W., and Zhu, Z. (2019). [Generalized High-Dimensional Trace Regression via Nuclear Norm Regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/1710.08083). *Annals of Statistics*, 47(5), 2633-2663.
